content.text: Passing a specific list of values as the second argument to
tells React exactly when to re-run the effect. After each render, React compares each element of the new array with the corresponding element from the previous render using
. If any element has changed, React runs the cleanup for the previous effect and then runs the new effect. If all elements are identical, the effect is skipped.

The flowchart shows the decision React makes after every committed render. With specific dependencies, the effect runs only when at least one dependency has changed — making it reactive to exactly the values it uses.
— essentially strict equality (===) with special handling for
) — to compare each dependency. This has important implications: primitive values (strings, numbers, booleans) compare by value, while objects, arrays, and functions compare by reference.
What Belongs in the Dependency Array

Every reactive value used inside the effect callback must appear in the dependency array. A reactive value is any variable that can change between renders and that the effect's behaviour depends on. React's ESLint rule
Props: any prop accessed inside the effect.
State variables: any useState value accessed inside the effect.
Variables derived from props or state that are declared in the component body.
Values from other hooks (useRef.current is an exception — see below).
What Does NOT Belong in the Array
Certain values are stable by design and do not need to be listed as dependencies:
setCount, setUser, etc. — React guarantees they are stable across renders.
The ref object is stable; .current is mutable but React does not track mutations. Reading ref.current inside an effect does not need to be a dep.
They never change, so they cannot trigger a re-run.
The most common source of unexpected effect re-runs is unstable references: an object, array, or function that is recreated on every render, making React think it is a new value.
Dependency Array vs Moving Logic Inside the Effect
Sometimes the cleanest fix for an unstable dependency is to move the computation inside the effect rather than adding it as a dep. This avoids stabilising the reference at the call site:
rule statically analyses your effect and reports every reactive value that you use but have not declared. It is the primary tool for catching incomplete dependency arrays.
Do not disable the lint rule.
silences the warning without fixing the underlying stale closure. Instead, restructure the code so the effect genuinely has only stable or necessary dependencies.
Yes — but may cause over-firing; use primitives
Yes — but stabilise with useCallback in parent
When testing a component whose effect has a dependency array, the key scenario to verify is that the effect re-runs exactly when the dependency changes and not on unrelated re-renders.
This test pattern — rerender with an unrelated prop change, then rerender with a dep change — verifies that the dependency array is correct and the effect is not over-firing.
When an object or function must be passed as a dependency, stabilise it with
so its reference only changes when its own inputs change:
Both approaches are correct. Moving the computation inside the effect is simpler; memoising is useful when
is also passed to other hooks or child components that need a stable reference.

When an effect performs several unrelated actions that depend on different values, split them into separate effects. Each effect runs independently when its own deps change, without disrupting the others.
The separated version means a theme change no longer cancels an in-progress fetch, and a userId change does not reset the theme class. Splitting by concern is almost always the right approach.
Sometimes what appears to require a dependency is actually derived state that should be computed during render, not inside an effect. Identifying this pattern reduces the number of effects needed:
, the effect is unnecessary. Derived values belong in the render function or in a
When a Dep Changes Too Often — useRef as an Escape Hatch

Occasionally a value is both reactive (the effect should eventually see its latest version) and too volatile to list as a dep (it changes on every render). A ref bridges this gap: update the ref every render, read the ref inside the effect.
hook. Until that stabilises, the ref approach is the idiomatic workaround.
reactive: it runs on mount and re-runs only when at least one dependency changes (compared via
Include every reactive value used inside the effect: props, state, context, derived values.
Exclude stable values: state setters, dispatch, ref objects, module-level constants.
Primitives compare by value — stable. Objects and functions compare by reference — may be unstable.
Unstable object/array/function dependencies cause over-firing; fix by using primitives or memoising.
The exhaustive-deps ESLint rule identifies missing dependencies — follow its guidance, do not disable it.
Moving computations inside the effect often eliminates complex dependency chains.
